answer:
Definition

Tool Calling (function calling) solves the problem of LLMs being limited to text generation. By defining tools (functions) the LLM can invoke, you extend its capabilities to query databases, call APIs, perform calculations, and take actions — turning a chatbot into an intelligent agent.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the Claude AI assistant uses tool calling to check an employee's real-time leave balance. Instead of guessing, the LLM calls getLeaveBalance(employeeId) which hits the Fastify API → PostgreSQL. The LLM then formats the factual data into a natural-language response.

Code Example

// Define tools for the LLM
const tools = [
  {
    name: 'getLeaveBalance',
    description: 'Get remaining leave days for an employee',
    input_schema: {
      type: 'object',
      properties: {
        employeeId: { type: 'string', description: 'Employee ID' },
        leaveType: { type: 'string', enum: ['sick', 'casual', 'earned'] },
      },
      required: ['employeeId'],
    },
  },
];

// Handle tool call
const response = await claude.messages.create({
  model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
  tools,
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: 'How many sick days do I have left?' }],
});

if (response.stop_reason === 'tool_use') {
  const toolCall = response.content.find(c => c.type === 'tool_use');
  const result = await executeToolCall(toolCall.name, toolCall.input);
  // Send result back to LLM for final response
}

Debugging Story

In the HR system, the LLM called getLeaveBalance with the user's name instead of their employee ID, causing a 404. Manoj fixed the tool schema by adding description: "The numeric employee ID, not the name" and added a resolver that maps names to IDs before the API call.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Exposing destructive tools (DELETE endpoints, approval actions) without confirmation — the LLM might invoke them based on ambiguous user intent.
Mitigation: Mark destructive tools as requiring user confirmation. Add a confirmation step before executing any state-changing tool.

Validation Strategy

  • Tool schema validation: ensure all tools have descriptions and typed parameters.
  • E2E test: send known queries → assert correct tools are called with correct parameters.
  • Security test: attempt to invoke destructive tools via prompt → assert confirmation is required.

Definition

The top 3 Tool Calling mistakes are: exposing destructive tools without safeguards, vague tool descriptions that confuse the LLM, and not handling tool call failures gracefully.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the AI assistant had a submitLeaveRequest tool. An employee said "I'm thinking about taking leave next week" and the LLM submitted a request immediately — interpreting intent as action. Manoj added a confirmation step: the tool returns a preview, and only after user confirmation does it actually submit.

Code Example

// ❌ BAD: Direct action without confirmation
const tools = [{
  name: 'submitLeave',
  description: 'Submit a leave request', // too vague
}];

// ✅ GOOD: Preview + confirm pattern
const tools = [
  {
    name: 'previewLeaveRequest',
    description: 'Show a preview of a leave request WITHOUT submitting. Use this first.',
  },
  {
    name: 'confirmLeaveRequest',
    description: 'Submit a previously previewed leave request. Only call after user confirms.',
  },
];

// Error handling
async function executeToolCall(name, input) {
  try {
    return await toolHandlers[name](input);
  } catch (err) {
    return {
      error: true,
      message: `Tool ${name} failed: ${err.message}. Please try again or ask differently.`,
    };
  }
}

Debugging Story

In the HR system, the LLM kept calling getLeaveBalance instead of getLeaveHistory because both descriptions mentioned "leave information." Manoj made descriptions specific: "Get remaining days count" vs "Get list of past leave requests with dates and status." The LLM immediately started selecting the correct tool.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Exposing 20+ tools to the LLM, causing confusion and slow response times as it evaluates all options.
Mitigation: Keep the active tool set small (< 10). Use a routing layer that selects relevant tools based on the user's intent before passing to the LLM.

Validation Strategy

  • Tool selection test: 50 queries → assert correct tool is chosen for each.
  • Error path test: simulate API failures → assert graceful error messages to user.
  • Confirmation test: ambiguous intent queries → assert preview is shown, not direct action.

Definition

A Context Window is the maximum number of tokens (words/subwords) an LLM can process in a single request — including the system prompt, conversation history, and response. Think of it as the model's working memory. Claude Sonnet has a 200K token window; GPT-4o has 128K. Exceeding it truncates earlier messages.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the AI assistant's conversation history grew unbounded — after 30 back-and-forth messages about leave policies, the context window hit the limit, and the model "forgot" the employee's name and leave type. Manoj implemented a sliding window that keeps the last 10 messages plus a system-prompt summary of key facts.

Code Example

// Context window manager
const MAX_TOKENS = 200_000;
const TOKEN_BUDGET = {
  system: 2_000,
  history: 4_000,
  context: 8_000,
  response: 4_000,
};

function buildMessages(history, ragDocs) {
  // Trim history to fit budget
  const trimmedHistory = trimToTokenLimit(history, TOKEN_BUDGET.history);
  
  // Summarize old messages if trimmed
  const summary = history.length > trimmedHistory.length
    ? `Previous context: Employee asked about ${extractTopics(history)}. `
    : '';

  return {
    system: systemPrompt + summary,
    messages: trimmedHistory,
    max_tokens: TOKEN_BUDGET.response,
  };
}

function trimToTokenLimit(messages, limit) {
  let tokens = 0;
  const kept = [];
  for (let i = messages.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
    tokens += countTokens(messages[i].content);
    if (tokens > limit) break;
    kept.unshift(messages[i]);
  }
  return kept;
}

Debugging Story

In the HR system, the assistant gave contradictory answers because the RAG context included 3 policy documents (15K tokens) that pushed conversation history out of the window. Manoj added a token budget allocator that reserves space for each section and truncates RAG documents if they exceed their budget — keeping the most relevant chunks based on similarity score.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Using the entire context window for input, leaving no space for the model's response — the output gets cut off mid-sentence.
Mitigation: Always reserve max_tokens for the response (e.g., 4K tokens). Calculate: available = window - system - history - max_tokens.

Validation Strategy

  • Token counting test: assert total prompt tokens never exceed 90% of context window.
  • Long conversation test: 50-message thread → assert no truncation errors and key facts are retained.
  • Budget audit: log token allocation per section in production to tune budgets.

Definition

The Context Window solves the "memory limit" problem of LLMs. Without context management, long conversations, large documents, or multiple tool results overflow the window — causing the model to silently drop important information and produce degraded answers.

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the AI tag-suggestion feature processes contact notes. Some contacts have 5+ years of notes (thousands of words). Without context window management, large notes were silently truncated, and the model generated tags based on incomplete information. Manoj added a summarization pre-step for notes exceeding 2K tokens.

Code Example

// Context window management for long contact notes
const MAX_NOTE_TOKENS = 2000;

async function suggestTags(contactNotes) {
  let processedNotes = contactNotes;

  // If notes exceed budget, summarize first
  if (countTokens(contactNotes) > MAX_NOTE_TOKENS) {
    const summary = await claude.messages.create({
      model: 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001', // fast, cheap model for summarization
      max_tokens: 500,
      messages: [{
        role: 'user',
        content: `Summarize these contact notes in 200 words, keeping key facts:
${contactNotes}`,
      }],
    });
    processedNotes = summary.content[0].text;
  }

  // Generate tags from manageable-sized notes
  return await generateTags(processedNotes);
}

Debugging Story

In ContactsHub, the summarization step used the same expensive model (Sonnet) as the tag generation — doubling API costs. Manoj switched summarization to Haiku (faster, cheaper) since summarization doesn't require deep reasoning. This cut AI costs by 40% while maintaining tag quality.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Using string.length as a proxy for token count — tokens ≠ characters. "hello world" is 2 tokens but 11 characters.
Mitigation: Use a proper tokenizer (@anthropic-ai/tokenizer or tiktoken) for accurate token counting.

Validation Strategy

  • Token budget test: send maximum-length notes → assert no API errors.
  • Quality test: compare tags from full notes vs summarized notes for 20 contacts → assert ≥ 90% overlap.
  • Cost monitoring: track token usage per API call → alert if average exceeds budget.

Definition

Retrieval Quality measures how well your RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) system fetches the right documents for a given query. Poor retrieval = feeding irrelevant context to the LLM = wrong answers. It's the foundation of any RAG pipeline — garbage in, garbage out.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the AI policy assistant retrieves relevant policy sections before answering. Initially, the vector search returned general HR sections instead of leave-specific ones. Manoj improved retrieval by chunking the policy document by section headers and adding keyword metadata to each chunk — boosting precision@3 from 60% to 92%.

Code Example

// Improved chunking strategy for policy document
function chunkPolicyDocument(document) {
  const sections = document.split(/\n## /); // Split by H2 headers

  return sections.map(section => {
    const [title, ...body] = section.split('\n');
    const text = body.join('\n').trim();
    return {
      title,
      text,
      keywords: extractKeywords(text), // BM25 metadata
      tokens: countTokens(text),
    };
  }).filter(chunk => chunk.tokens > 50); // Skip tiny sections
}

// Hybrid search: vector + keyword
async function hybridSearch(query, topK = 3) {
  const vectorResults = await vectorStore.similaritySearch(query, topK * 2);
  const keywordResults = await bm25Search(query, topK * 2);

  // Reciprocal rank fusion
  const merged = reciprocalRankFusion(vectorResults, keywordResults);
  return merged.slice(0, topK);
}

Debugging Story

In the HR system, "How do I apply for maternity leave?" returned chunks about sick leave because the vector embeddings for "maternity" and "sick" were close in the embedding space (both relate to health). Manoj added keyword boosting — if the query contains "maternity," chunks with that exact word get a score boost, fixing the retrieval.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Chunking documents by fixed character count (e.g., every 500 chars) breaks context mid-sentence, ruining retrieval quality.
Mitigation: Chunk by semantic boundaries — section headers, paragraphs, or topic shifts. Include overlap (50-100 tokens) between chunks.

Validation Strategy

  • Precision@3 test: 50 labeled queries with known relevant chunks → measure retrieval accuracy.
  • Chunk quality audit: review 20 random chunks for completeness and coherence.
  • End-to-end accuracy: measure final answer quality with good vs bad retrieval → quantify impact.

Definition

Retrieval Quality solves the "wrong context" problem in RAG pipelines. Even with a perfect LLM, if you feed it irrelevant documents, the answer will be wrong or hallucinated. Retrieval quality ensures the right information reaches the model before generation.

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, a search feature lets users find contacts by natural language ("find my college friends in Bangalore"). The retrieval system searches contact notes and tags using hybrid search (vector similarity + keyword match). Initially, "college friends" returned work colleagues because the embedding model treated "college" and "colleagues" as similar.

Code Example

// Natural language contact search
async function searchContacts(query) {
  // Step 1: Vector search on contact embeddings
  const vectorHits = await vectorStore.search(query, { topK: 10 });

  // Step 2: Keyword search on tags and notes
  const keywords = extractKeywords(query); // ["college", "friends", "bangalore"]
  const keywordHits = await db.contacts.find({
    $or: [
      { tags: { $in: keywords } },
      { notes: { $regex: keywords.join('|'), $options: 'i' } },
      { city: { $regex: 'bangalore', $options: 'i' } },
    ],
  });

  // Step 3: Merge and re-rank
  const merged = mergeResults(vectorHits, keywordHits);
  return merged.slice(0, 5);
}

Debugging Story

In ContactsHub, the search for "contacts added last month" returned random results because the embedding model doesn't understand temporal queries. Manoj added a pre-processing step that detects time-based queries and routes them to a date-range filter instead of semantic search — temporal queries bypass the vector store entirely.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Using a generic embedding model for domain-specific content — it may not distinguish domain terms ("sick leave" vs "casual leave" both embed as "time off").
Mitigation: Fine-tune embeddings on domain data, or use hybrid search with keyword boosting for critical domain terms.

Validation Strategy

  • Search relevance test: 30 natural-language queries → human-rate top-5 results for relevance.
  • Temporal query test: 10 time-based queries → assert date-range filter is used, not vector search.
  • A/B test: vector-only vs hybrid search → measure click-through rate on search results.
